relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus remains in Galilee because Judea has become dangerous.
The feast of Booths draws near.
His brothers urge public display in Judea.
They confuse revelation with publicity.
Even his brothers do not yet trust.
Jesus moves by appointed time, not pressure.
The world hates the witness that exposes harmful works.
His time is not yet fulfilled.
He remains in Galilee.
He goes to the feast hiddenly, not as spectacle.
The authorities seek him.
The crowd divides over whether he is good or misleading.
Fear keeps speech hidden.
Mid-feast, Jesus teaches in the temple.
His knowledge of writings astonishes them.
His teaching belongs to the Sender.
Doing the Divine will becomes the path to knowing the teaching.
Truth seeks the Sender’s glory, not self-glory.
Moses and the Law expose the murder-intent.
The crowd dismisses the danger as spirit-trouble.
One healing work has made them marvel.
Circumcision on Sabbath becomes the comparison.
If Sabbath can receive circumcision, it can receive whole-human healing.
Appearance-judgment must yield to just judgment.
Jerusalemites recognize the threat against him.
His open speech raises the Messiah question.
Known origin becomes their obstacle.
Jesus cries out that they know surface origin but not the true Sender.
He knows the Sender because he is from the Sender.
They cannot seize him because the hour has not come.
Many trust through signs and wonder if Messiah could do more.
Officers are sent to seize him.
Jesus speaks of little time and return to the Sender.
They will seek and not find where he is.
They misunderstand through geography and diaspora.
His word remains puzzling to them.
On the great feast day, thirst is invited to come and drink.
Trust becomes rivers of living water from within.
The living-water rivers point to Spirit after glorification.
Some hear the words and name him the Prophet.
Others name Messiah, while others object from Galilee.
Scripture about David and Bethlehem becomes argument.
The crowd is divided because of him.
Some want to seize him, but no hands are laid.
The officers return empty-handed.
They say no human has ever spoken like this.
The Pharisees accuse them of being led astray.
They appeal to elite disbelief.
The crowd is dismissed as cursed for not knowing the Law.
Nicodemus speaks from within the circle.
He asks whether Law judges before hearing and knowing.
They answer him with Galilee contempt.
This line begins the later textual tradition leading into the woman-accusation story.
